issue_title: Improve support for arbitrary license names
issue_body:
The current implementation has an arbitrary limitation that an expression with `and`, `or` or `with` keywords that are part of a license name and not part a keyword of the expression would not parse nor resolve correctly.

Now this limitation does not need to exist as when a list of LicenseRef is provided that is otherwise not ambiguous (e.g. with no name resolving to more than one license key) the parsing is not ambiguous.

So I propose to update this to handle properly expressions with such licenses. This would work only when resolution on parsing is requested or when resolution is requested AND that license references are provided.

issue_title: Online Documentation
issue_body:
While working with goose before participating in the development of goose3, I found that the documentation is lacking. Currently, the only documentation is the README file. Unfortunately, this does not contain all the information about how to configure goose. 

There are a few options: 

- Use something like [sphinx autodocs](http://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/stable/) that can then be uploaded to [readthedocs](https://readthedocs.org/) and can use the doc-string to automatically build the documentation 
- [MkDocs](http://www.mkdocs.org/) can also be uploaded to [readthedocs](https://readthedocs.org/). 
- Hard coded HTML files

Thoughts and comments would be great. I am happy to help start the documentation if others find this to be helpful.

issue_title: Compiler error with return types in PYBIND11_OVERLOAD
pybind11 uses deprecated PyThread_set_key_value API
issue_body:
## Issue description

As of Python 3.7, the PyThread_* API was deprecated and superceded by the Thread Specific Storage API.

Details from Python documentation:
* https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/init.html#thread-specific-storage-tss-api
* https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0539/

Compiling against Python 3.7 will produce numerous deprecation warnings for every file that includes pybind11 headers. Despite this, everything seems to be working okay. 

To suppress the warnings, pybind11 should select the new API on Python 3.7.

issue_title: [Network] Support Application Gateway API 2017-06-01
issue_body:
### Description
We are pushing to release few features for application gateway in the early July release for CLI.
* [Swagger changes](https://github.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/pull/1285) are in for these features. 
* [Powershell Diff](https://github.com/Azure/azure-powershell/pull/4177/files)
**Note**: We are running late as NRP update is running late and hope that it gets over by 27 June.

### Summary of Changes
1)	ApplicationGateway Ssl Policy custom cipher suites support [new properties added to Sslpolicy Property of ApplciationGatewayPropertiesFormat]
2)	Get AvailableSslOptions api [new resource ApplicationGatewayAvailableSslOptions and child resource ApplicationGatewayPredefinedPolicy]
3)	Redirection support [new child resource ApplicationGatewayRedirectConfiguration for Application Gateway, new properties in UrlPathMap, PathRules and RequestRoutingRule]
4)	Azure Websites feature support [new properties in ApplicationGatewayBackendHttpSettingsPropertiesFormat, ApplicationGatewayProbePropertiesFormat, schema for property ApplicationGatewayProbeHealthResponseMatch]

The reference documentation and any other related documentation for performing this in CLI does not clearly specify the value required.  

**To Reproduce**
If following the documentation, I would enter the following:  

az monitor diagnostic-settings create --name NSGDiagnostics \

    --workspace DefaultWorkspaceSpace1999 \

It returns the following error:  Operation failed [Code: “resourcenotfound”].  It isn't clear what the exact issue is when the error is returned, which is another frustrating by-byproduct of the issue.

Install Method CLI version 2.0

**Additional context**
Add any other context about the problem here.

issue_title: The Deployments of hosted_service is NULL? Could you please fix that
Generated Python 3 modules has syntax errors, causing a fall back to Python 2 modules
issue_body:
I'm assuming recent changes have made the code generator output two versions of the code, one for Python 2 and another for Python 3.

However, the Python 3 generated code contains a recurring pattern where a parent class's __init__ method is called with a malformed parameter list starting with a comma (`__init__(, arg1, arg2, etc)`, causing a syntax error. Here are some examples:

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/72d71b30af36e65b333e2225ce8732afa53a4ec4/azure-mgmt-monitor/azure/mgmt/monitor/models/rule_email_action_py3.py#L43

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/72d71b30af36e65b333e2225ce8732afa53a4ec4/azure-mgmt-monitor/azure/mgmt/monitor/models/rule_webhook_action_py3.py#L42

https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/72d71b30af36e65b333e2225ce8732afa53a4ec4/azure-mgmt-monitor/azure/mgmt/monitor/models/diagnostic_settings_category_resource_py3.py#L46

The `__init__` module of `azure.mgmt.monitor.models` uses a massive try/except block to import all the `_py3` modules, and if a SyntaxError or ImportError is caught it imports the Python 2 modules instead.

I'm not privy to how the code generator works and may be missing something, but IMO a cleaner way to test the version of Python IMO would be to use an if statement like `import sys; if sys.version[0] >= '3':` instead of the try / except. That way automated testing could ensure that no import errors or syntax errors occur at all.

On a different note it's a pleasant surprise to see a major cloud provider supporting Python 3 type annotations in its SDK. :)

issue_title: When building cross-platform pex, stymied by abi tag for linux packages
issue_body:
I'm using pants 1.0.0 to build a cross platform pex (for pants itself)
The version of pex used by pants is pex==1.1.2

We created some wheels with `pip --wheel ` and they look like:

```
$ ls *linux*
lmdb-0.89-cp27-cp27mu-linux_x86_64.whl          
pywatchman-1.3.0-cp27-cp27mu-linux_x86_64.whl  
...
```

But when we try to link in these wheels with our pants target, the pex resolver can't find them:

```
Exception message: Could not get distribution for SourcePackage(u'file:///Users/zundel/Development/java/.pants.d/python-setup/resolved_requirements/CPython-2.7.10/PyYAML-3.11.tar.gz') on platform linux-x86_64.
```

On a hunch, I found that if I rename the wheels to remove the cp27mu field (This is the 'abi' tag on the wheel as per: PEP-427[1]

```
lmdb-0.89-cp27-none-linux_x86_64.whl          
pywatchman-1.3.0-cp27-none-linux_x86_64.whl 
```

Now pex can resolve them.  Is this expected behavior?  If so:

Is there something I can do in pants/pex to allow it to use wheels with this ABI tag?
Does anyone know how to get pip to generate these wheels with an abi tag of `none` (other than just renaming them)?

Here's a snippet from the PEP-425[2] doc. 

> Why is the ABI tag (the second tag) sometimes "none" in the reference implementation?
> Since Python 2 does not have an easy way to get to the SOABI (the concept comes from newer versions of Python 3) the reference implentation at the time of writing guesses "none". Ideally it would detect "py27(d|m|u)" analagous to newer versions of Python, but in the meantime "none" is a good enough way to say "don't know".

Maybe the weird thing is that this wheel that got generated has both an `m` and a `u` suffix?  In ~/.pex/install I see that when pex caches them, it uses `cp27m`:

```
ls -d ~/.pex/install/*cp27m*
/Users/zundel/.pex/install/PyYAML-3.11-cp27-cp27m-linux_x86_64.whl.1ac921760b77435dc948b81372d7a03034ef2795
/Users/zundel/.pex/install/pywatchman-1.3.0-cp27-cp27m-linux_x86_64.whl.26077f5592aa3f150a6101aba73e3f5c3146b416
/Users/zundel/.pex/install/setproctitle-1.1.9-cp27-cp27m-linux_x86_64.whl.74786c3aa705b664033287ed98a888031299acbe
```

[1]:https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0427
[2]:https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0425/

issue_title: Buildcache can break node_module targets
issue_body:
TarballArtifact in artifact.py uses the sets the "dereference" option from the tarfile Python module (https://docs.python.org/2/library/tarfile.html#tarfile-objects) to True. See https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/cache/artifact.py#L99.

This option resolves symlinks, turning them into copies of the things they pointed to. In node_module targets, this means the symlinks under "<target dir>/node_modules/.bin" (such as "mocha -> ../mocha/bin/mocha") are resolved into copies of the executables files they point to. This breaks, for example, the common JS testing framework Mocha, which expects relative paths to be relative to its real bin directory (see https://github.com/mochajs/mocha/blob/master/bin/_mocha#L12).

For node_module targets, you can get around this with:
```
[cache.resolve.npm]
read: False
write: False
```

But talking to @patricklaw and @stuhood, it sounds like we can just drop the "'dereference': True" for all caching.

issue_title: `pants server` entries don't reflect the proper cli invocation when executed through pantsd.
Store remote process execution's inline stdout and stderr in the store
issue_body:
In remote execution, the remote server may return stdout and stderr as digests, or as inline bytes: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/724b7502be4074cb8d9f4c4d137c1921d1b6cb73/3rdparty/protobuf/googleapis/google/devtools/remoteexecution/v1test/remote_execution.proto#L565-L615

We generally use the Store (https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/724b7502be4074cb8d9f4c4d137c1921d1b6cb73/src/rust/engine/fs/src/store.rs) to keep copies of all bytes which we may want to reference in the future.

These functions: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/724b7502be4074cb8d9f4c4d137c1921d1b6cb73/src/rust/engine/process_execution/src/remote.rs#L331-L392 fetch the digests (and stores them in the local store as a side-effect) if they were returned as digests. If the stdout/stderr were returned inline, it just returns the bytes, without storing the bytes in the store.

We should make it so that if stdout_raw/stderr_raw were set, we store these bytes in the store so that they can be looked up in the future if needed.

issue_title: Products as objects; replace string representation of Products with objects
issue_body:
From email conversations:

We want to use opaque (they extend object - no particular relationship until we see a need) types, ie: CompileClasspath, RuntimeClasspath, ClassfileMapping, MarkdownMapping,  etc.  Each of which is a type exported by a backend.  The implication is that if, for example, you write a new jvm task that needs the RuntimeClasspath, you'll need a BUILD/import dep on the pants-provided jvm backend.

issue_title: Graph invalidation
issue_body: This is a followup to #2946. What "native" means, really, is that graph invalidation is aware that particular graph nodes represent file operations. So graph invalidation is the only thing that needs to identify and special case for filesystem operations. Optionally, we could also create actual `Node` subclasses for these operations.

issue_title: Changing state of started or joined application is deprecated....grrrr
issue_body:
## Long story short

I very often want to set items on "frozen" app. I'm getting depreciation warnings for this and in future (presumably) this will fail outright. This is very frustrating.

This is made worse by pytest 3.1 which prints all warnings making this harder to ignore.

## Expected behaviour

```python
app['database'] = await create_my_database(...)
```

Should not cause deprecation warnings.

## Actual behaviour

I'm getting warnings in lots of setups which I would have thought sensible.

Examples:

**in unit tests** setting up a secondary server:

```python
@pytest.fixture
def mock_external_server(loop, test_server):
    app = Application()
    app.router.add_post('/whatever.json', handler)
    server = loop.run_until_complete(test_server(app))
    app['server_name'] = f'http://localhost:{server.port}'
    return server
```

This causes a warning because I'm setting the `server_name` item on an app which is started.

**using startup scripts:**
```python
async def startup(app: web.Application):
    app['pg_engine'] = await create_engine(pg_dsn(app['database']), loop=app.loop)

...

app = web.Application()
app.on_startup.append(startup)
```
(real life example [here](https://github.com/tutorcruncher/socket-server/blob/master/tcsocket/app/main.py#L15-L20))

This again causes warnings setting `pg_engine` in a startup script.

Surely this warning is wrong? If not what workaround should I use?

The only solution I can think of is an ugly bodge:

```python
async def startup(app: web.Application):
    app['data']['pg_engine'] = await create_engine(pg_dsn(app['database']), loop=app.loop)

...

app = web.Application(middlewares=middleware)
app['data'] = {}
```

issue_title: [Investigation] Leaking of timeout task cancellation
issue_body:
In one of our applications we use aiobotocore, which layers on top of aiohttp.  I have S3 worker processes that have ~50 aiobotocore tasks working in parallel (`head_object`, `get_object`, etc).

I've noticed w/in the last month that I started getting spurious `CancelledError`s from aiohttp.  I've done some debugging and it seems there's something broken with how the `CeilTimeout` works in `ClientSession._request`.  In my particular scenario the call structure looks like:

```
outer_task
    aiobotocore_method
       while num_retries:
           try:

issue_title: Implement learningRadius parameter in Temporal Memory
Add Windows cmd line support alongside bash
Partition (sequence) ID support
issue_body:
Github issue corresponding to https://jira.numenta.com/browse/RES-112

We want to be able to pass the sequence ID info throughout the network; this is useful for debugging purposes. The [partitionID in the KNNClassifier|https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/algorithms/KNNClassifier.py#L359] is supposed to do this, but it's not clear this works. So the task is to first test to see if this is functioning properly. Then we need to pass the sequence (partition) ID number throughout the network. The LanguageSensor already has an output 

["sequenceIdOut"|https://github.com/numenta/nupic.research/blob/master/htmresearch/regions/LanguageSensor.py#L101], but this needs to be linked to the subsequent regions in the network. This requires (i) that these regions (SP, TM, TP, KNN, and CLA) have inputs for "sequenceIdIn", and (ii) that the links are created by the network factory...
i. add to the the spec:
          sequenceIdIn=dict(
            description="Sequence ID",
            dataType='UInt64',
            count=1,
            required=False,
            regionLevel=True,
            isDefaultInput=False,
            requireSplitterMap=False)
ii. add the following in the network factory's linking method:
  network.link(sensorRegionName, currentRegionName, "UniformLink", "",
               srcOutput="sequenceIdOut", destInput="sequenceIdIn")

issue_title: Model instance overwritten after commit.
issue_body:
Hi, I'm using the latest version of the repo with sa v. 1.0.12. I'm noticing some strange behavior if I define a model with an identity column. Here's an example:

```Python
import os

import sqlalchemy as sa
from sqlalchemy.ext import declarative

Base = declarative.declarative_base()


class IdentityExample(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'identity_example'

    id = sa.Column(sa.Integer, primary_key=True, info={'identity': (0, 1)})
    name = sa.Column(sa.String)


engine = sa.create_engine(os.environ['REDSHIFT_URL'])
Base.metadata.drop_all(engine)
Base.metadata.create_all(engine)
RedshiftSession = sa.orm.sessionmaker()
session = RedshiftSession(bind=engine)

example_1 = IdentityExample(name='Example #1')
session.add(example_1)
print("EXAMPLE 1 BEFORE: ", example_1.id, example_1.name)
session.commit()
print("EXAMPLE 1 AFTER: ", example_1.id, example_1.name)

example_2 = IdentityExample(name='Example #2')
session.add(example_2)
print("EXAMPLE 2 BEFORE: ", example_2.id, example_2.name)
session.commit()
print("EXAMPLE 2 AFTER: ", example_2.id, example_2.name)
```

I'd expect to see output like this:

```
EXAMPLE 1 BEFORE:  None Example #1
EXAMPLE 1 AFTER:  0 Example #1
EXAMPLE 2 BEFORE:  None Example #2
EXAMPLE 2 AFTER:  1 Example #2
```

Instead I see this:

```
EXAMPLE 1 BEFORE:  None Example #1
EXAMPLE 1 AFTER:  0 Example #1
EXAMPLE 2 BEFORE:  None Example #2
EXAMPLE 2 AFTER:  0 Example #1
```

Am I making some mistake, or is this a bug? Thanks!

issue_title: How to specify attributes (such as dump_only,...) on Nested fields?
Marshmallow-schema'd body parameters lose 'required' and 'description' field spec
issue_body:
Consider this code:

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields
from apispec import APISpec

spec = APISpec(
        title='bug',
        version="0.0.1",
        plugins=(
            'apispec.ext.marshmallow',
        )
    )

class SomeBodyParam(Schema):
    name = fields.String(required=True)
    city = fields.String()
    age = fields.Integer()

spec.add_path(
    'foo/',
    {
        'post': {
            'parameters': [
                { 'name': 'some body param',
                  'in': 'body',
                  'schema': SomeBodyParam(),
                  'description': 'Description disapears and so does required',
                  'required': True
                  }
            ]
        }
    }
)

spec.to_dict()
```

The output is:

```
{'definitions': {},
 'info': {'title': 'bug', 'version': '0.0.1'},
 'parameters': {},
 'paths': OrderedDict([('foo/',
               {'post': {'parameters': [{'in': 'body',
                   'name': 'body',
                   'required': False,
                   'schema': {'properties': {'name': {'type': 'string'}, 'city': {'type': 'string'}, 'age': {'type': 'integer', 'format': 'int32'}},
                    'required': ['name'],
                    'type': 'object'}}]}})]),
 'swagger': '2.0',
 'tags': []}
```

Bug:
- The `required` field switched from True to False
- The `description` field got lost

The `name` parameter also gets renamed, but I believe this is per-spec.

I believe the cause is here: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/0.27.0/apispec/ext/marshmallow/__init__.py#L148

Adding a `, required=parameter['required'] if 'required' in parameter else False` ought to fix the required bit.  Do something similar for `description`, but need to add it to [swagger.py:fields2parameters()](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/apispec/blob/0.27.0/apispec/ext/marshmallow/swagger.py#L367) signature and include it in the dict constructor in there.

That sound like a reasonable approach?

issue_title: The removal of load_from/dump_to in 3.x is not documented
issue_body:
So if I'm getting this right, #717 appears to remove `load_from` (replacing it with `data_key`, new) and `dump_to` (replacing it with `attribute`, already existing).

This took me a bit too long to figure out and the fact that the default docs version is "latest", an unreleased 3.x, didn't help. I'm still using 2.x and the lack of mention that `load_from` and `dump_to` ever existed made me think that I must have messed up somewhere else when i tried to use `data_key` there.

issue_title: numexpr leaves arrays in C- nor Fortran ordering
issue_body:
This is more of a question, rather than an issue, but I couldn't find a reference to a users list, so I hope it's ok here.

After rewriting code to use numexpr, I noticed that is slowed down some numba code elsewhere significantly, unless I forced the output to be in C contiguous form. Upon checking the flags, I noticed that the array output wasn't F contiguous either. I can definitely see how this would influence numba code, but would you expect later numpy code to experience a similar slowdown? Given the adage that numpy performs best when given C contiguous arrays...

issue_title: OECD `test_get_tourism` test fails (apparent data change).
issue_body:
For the past couple of weeks, the OECD `test_get_tourism` test has been failing. The last tests for #157 (on January 11) passed, but the ones after that time seem to have failed reliably.

If you run the data pull from the test, it returns real data.

```
>>> import pandas_datareader as pdr
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> df = pdr.DataReader('TOURISM_INBOUND', 'oecd', start=datetime(2005, 1, 1),
...                     end=datetime(2012, 1, 1))
>>> df
Country                       Australia                                 \
Variable   Total international arrivals   China United Kingdom   Japan   
Year                                                                     
2008-01-01                      5512313  350155         686715  450133   
2009-01-01                      5490229  356239         676822  348465   
2010-01-01                      5790260  445855         660335  390555   
2011-01-01                      5770902  533370         622254  325740   
2012-01-01                      6032317  618818         608319  348054   

Country                                                                     \
Variable   New Zealand United States Switzerland and Liechtenstein Germany   
Year                                                                         
2008-01-01     1099442        448836                           NaN     NaN   
2009-01-01     1094418        472233                           NaN     NaN   
2010-01-01     1145959        462941                           NaN     NaN   
2011-01-01     1156307        447148                           NaN     NaN   
2012-01-01     1184656        470858                           NaN     NaN   

Country                         ...    Philippines                     \
Variable   Italy Netherlands    ...        Denmark Thailand Singapore   
Year                            ...                                     
2008-01-01   NaN         NaN    ...            NaN      NaN       NaN   
2009-01-01   NaN         NaN    ...            NaN      NaN       NaN   
2010-01-01   NaN         NaN    ...            NaN      NaN       NaN   
2011-01-01   NaN         NaN    ...            NaN      NaN       NaN   
2012-01-01   NaN         NaN    ...            NaN      NaN       NaN   

Country                                                               \
Variable   Malaysia Private accommodation Specialised establishments   
Year                                                                   
2008-01-01      NaN                   NaN                        NaN   
2009-01-01      NaN                   NaN                        NaN   
2010-01-01      NaN                   NaN                        NaN   
2011-01-01      NaN                   NaN                        NaN   
2012-01-01      NaN                   NaN                        NaN   

Country                                            
Variable   Venezuela Ecuador Bangladesh Sri Lanka  
Year                                               
2008-01-01       NaN     NaN        NaN       NaN  
2009-01-01       NaN     NaN        NaN       NaN  
2010-01-01       NaN     NaN        NaN       NaN  
2011-01-01       NaN     NaN        NaN       NaN  
2012-01-01       NaN     NaN        NaN       NaN  

[5 rows x 4368 columns]
>>> df['United States']['Total international arrivals']
Year
2008-01-01    175702309
2009-01-01    160507417
2010-01-01    164079732
2011-01-01    167600277
2012-01-01    171320408
Name: Total international arrivals, dtype: float64
```

However, when comparing it to the test, three main issues stand out.

1. The precision of the stats appears to have changed from thousands (test) to individuals.
2. The stats also appear to have been updated (not just rounded), as the comparable U.S. number for 2008 is `175632.0` in the test and `175702309` in the data.
3. The time coverage now appears to start at 2008, though the tests are written assuming 2005.

These observations and the timing of the test failure seem to fit with the recent update (January 2016) noted by and current data available from [OECD](https://stats.oecd.org/Index.aspx?DataSetCode=TOURISM_INBOUND#).

If we're fine with the testing approach, I should be able to update the test to have it pass when pulling the new data.

issue_title: Publish virtualchain documentation
Invalid config file name error is ignored
issue_body: When an invalid file name is used with the --config option studio run will continue without any indication there is a problem.

issue_title: Make mockup modal events usable outside a pattern
issue_body:
The modal pattern provide a lot of callback events like `onSuccess`, `onError`, ... but they are not usable with simple HTML markup.
This is a huge limit IMHO.

I think this patterns (*hint*: every pattern probably) should do the same already implemented by the sortable pattern, [where the callback can be a string](https://github.com/plone/mockup/blob/463f57f8042b2c6ed72c4c6b212a8de8edb16c89/mockup/patterns/sortable/pattern.js#L113), and in this case a global function is called.

This will make patterns usage easier without the amount of boilerplate required by implementing other patterns or manually use JS.

issue_title: plone.api.portal.get_registry_record dumps all registry entries.
issue_body:
plone.api.portal.get_registry_record dumps all registry entries when a non-existing key is passed in.
In Plone 5 this is a list of 1500+ items, including all plone.resources settings etc.

The list shown should be reduced to something like
```
records = [_name for _name, record in registry.records.items() if name.lower() in _name.lower()]
```

issue_body: in json responses--careful of what we dump

issue_title: Update readthedocs links

issue_title: Convert all use of posixpath.join to urljoin
issue_body:
Re: https://github.com/cloudant/python-cloudant/pull/120/files/23b5a04d1b227d9d5a2331491d9d1158c8e42dd1#r57008917

The library uses posixpath.join throughout to construct URLs, we should instead use `urljoin` from `urlparse` in Py2 and `urllib.parse` in Py3.  This change should be applied throughout the library.

[Python 2 urlparse.urljoin](https://docs.python.org/2/library/urlparse.html#urlparse.urljoin)
[Python3 urllib.parse.urljoin](https://docs.python.org/3.2/library/urllib.parse.html#urllib.parse.urljoin)

issue_title: Model object accessible via old class after class changed raises TypeError
issue_body:
[This Django application demonstrates a bug in django-polymorphic.](https://github.com/nmoskopp/djangoPolymorphicTestcase)

The application `djangoPolymorphicTestcase` has three model classes:
* `Base`, a `polymorphic.models.PolymorphicModel`
* `VariantA`, which inherits from `Base`
* `VariantB`, which inherits from `Base`

The test creates a `VariantA` object with primary key of `DUPLICATE`.
It then creates a `VariantB` object with a primary key of `DUPLICATE`.
As a result we do not have two objects, but a single `VariantB` object.
Accessing it via `VariantA.objects()` is possible, but throws `TypeError`.

issue_title: TypeError: issubclass() arg 2 must be a class or tuple of classes
issue_body:
Good day,
    I may be barking up the wrong tree, but I'm grasping at straws.  This may be an issue with django_polymorphic, but it could just as easily be "Sorry shaunbrady, your princess is in another castle".  With some new tests I have been writing, celery (https://github.com/RentMethod/django-celery-testworker/ to be specific) produces this error:

```
2014-10-27 10:31:48,679: WARNING/MainProcess] celery@excalibur ready.
[2014-10-27 10:32:53,698: ERROR/Worker-3] StartSessionTask for session ef5d6af8-eb1b-4f62-a7ca-a779119ebeb0 failed: issubclass() arg 2 mu
st be a class or tuple of classes
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/sbrady/.virtualenvs/site-awesim/src/session-epi/session_epi/tasks.py", line 52, in run
    session = EpiSession.objects.get(uuid=uuid)
  File "/home/sbrady/.virtualenvs/site-awesim/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/models/manager.py", line 151, in get
    return self.get_queryset().get(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/sbrady/.virtualenvs/site-awesim/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/models/query.py", line 304, in get
    num = len(clone)
  File "/home/sbrady/.virtualenvs/site-awesim/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/models/query.py", line 77, in __len__
    self._fetch_all()
  File "/home/sbrady/.virtualenvs/site-awesim/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/models/query.py", line 857, in _fetch_all
    self._result_cache = list(self.iterator())
  File "/home/sbrady/.virtualenvs/site-awesim/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/polymorphic/query.py", line 280, in iterator
    real_results = self._get_real_instances(base_result_objects)
  File "/home/sbrady/.virtualenvs/site-awesim/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/polymorphic/query.py", line 176, in _get_real_instances
    real_concrete_class = base_object.get_real_instance_class()
  File "/home/sbrady/.virtualenvs/site-awesim/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/polymorphic/polymorphic_model.py", line 116, in get_real_instance_class
    and not issubclass(model, self.__class__._meta.proxy_for_model):
TypeError: issubclass() arg 2 must be a class or tuple of classes
```
Possibly of interest, the tests work if I run one or two at a time, so a timing or cache issue makes sense.

Traveling to line 115 of polymorphic_model.py, I put some debugging in right before:
```
        if model is not None \
        and not issubclass(model, self.__class__) \
        and not issubclass(model, self.__class__._meta.proxy_for_model):
```
The code:
```
        print "model is {0}".format(model)
        print "self.__class__ is {0}".format(self.__class__)                                                  
        print "self.__class__._meta.proxy_for_model is {0}".format(self.__class__._meta.proxy_for_model)
```

Produces:
```
[2014-10-27 10:43:03,892: WARNING/Worker-4] model is <class 'session_epi.models.EpiApplicationConfiguration'>
[2014-10-27 10:43:03,892: WARNING/Worker-4] self.__class__ is <class 'session_epi.models.EpiSession'>
[2014-10-27 10:43:03,892: WARNING/Worker-4] self.__class__._meta.proxy_for_model is None
```

session_epi.models.EpiApplicationConfiguration is a model in the same app as EpiSession, but they do not inherit from each other.  They share a few mixins and are both model.Models, but are otherwise separate.  As well EpiApplicationConfiguration is not used any where near the calling code.

Adding 
```
ContentType.objects.clear_cache()
```
immediately above the line
```
model = ContentType.objects.get_for_id(self.polymorphic_ctype_id).model_class()
```
fixes the issue; all four tests complete just fine.  No debugging output is sent.  I believe this implies that AttributeError was raised and None was returned.  

From https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.6/ref/contrib/contenttypes/#django.contrib.contenttypes.models.ContentTypeManager.clear_cache
it indicates one should not have to clear the cache.  I'm not sure if that applies to apps like django_polymorphic or to higher level applications.  Let me know if you think something else should be doing this work for us, if there is a better way to make sure this cache is clear, or if this only coincidentally fixes the issue.  

We are using Django 1.6.5 and django_polymorphic 0.5.6 from pip.  Upgrading to django_polymorphic 0.6 does not fix the issue.  

Thanks for any help.  I can provide you with any more information you want.  

SB

issue_title: Make enums more robust and dev-friendly
issue_body:
Right now, in the inputs the API users have two ways of sending the input value of an enum variable:

- Send the value index in the enum (e.g. `2`), which is not explicit
- Send the label, which is not robust (e.g. `"Propriétaire (non accédant) du logement"`) which is not robust

Also, coding formulas using enum is not really writer-friendly:

```py
coeff_meuble = where(statut_occupation_logement == 5, 2 / 3, 1)  # Coeff de 2/3 pour les meublés
```

issue_title: Handle input errors in the Web API

EDIT by @fpagnoux:

As a user of the OpenFisca web API who sends a wrong input,
I get a clear message that explicits why my input is not valid,
So that I can fix it easily

-------------------

Follows openfisca/openfisca-france#716

Problem is explained in the referenced issue.

We could catch `ValueError` around [this line](https://github.com/openfisca/openfisca-web-api/blob/433e8ded43a3669cee577b53b20518a53aa8be79/openfisca_web_api/controllers/calculate.py#L82), but `ValueError` is not enough specific and this could lead to abusive exception catches.

CC @fpagnoux

- test that error

issue_title: Boolean envvars not being coerced
issue_body:
I believe that the boolean environment variables are not being correctly type coerced.

For example, in `disable_action`:
https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/c8b8cb22618bb9836ed556062274c601406171e2/curator/validators/options.py#L30

If I use an envvar set as `"true"` or `"false"` (or `"True"` or `"False"`), it will say that it requires an int.
If I use an envvar set as `"0"` or `"1"`, it will also say that it requires an int.

I think that this is a problem with the `Coerce(bool)` validator, but I'm not completely sure (I never used Voluptuous).

Nevertheless, Voluptuous supports a [Boolean](https://alecthomas.github.io/voluptuous/docs/_build/html/voluptuous.html?highlight=coerce#voluptuous.validators.Boolean) validator. Maybe it would be better to use that one.

issue_title: Use python >= 2.7.9 in Dockerfile to avoid SSL Warning 'SNIMissingWarning'
issue_body:
## For usage questions and help
Please create a topic at https://discuss.elastic.co/c/elasticsearch

Perhaps a topic there already has an answer for you!

## To submit a bug or report an issue
<!--- Provide a general summary of the issue in the Title above -->
Building curator with the provided Dockerfile currently leads to python SSL warnings: 'SNIMissingWarning', when connecting to an SSL secured ES database.

### Expected Behavior
<!--- Tell us what should happen -->

### Actual Behavior
<!--- Tell us what happens instead of the expected behavior -->

### Steps to Reproduce the Problem
<!--- Provide a link to a live example, or an unambiguous set of steps to -->
<!--- reproduce this bug. Include code to reproduce, if relevant -->
  1.
  1.
  1.

### Specifications

  - Version: curator 5.5.1
  - Platform: Linux/Docker
  - Subsystem:


## Context (Environment)
<!--- How has this issue affected you? What are you trying to accomplish? -->
<!--- Providing context helps us come up with a solution that is most useful in the real world -->

<!--- Provide a general summary of the issue in the Title above -->

## Detailed Description
<!--- Provide a detailed description of the change or addition you are proposing -->
Dockerfile currently uses python 2.7.8 (maybe for good reason, but not obvious for me).
This leads to python SSL warnings: 'SNIMissingWarning', when using curator within the resulting docker image and connecting to an SSL secured ES database:

```
SNIMissingWarning: An HTTPS request has been made, but the SNI (Subject Name Indication) extension to TLS is not available on this platform. This may cause the server to present an incorrect TLS certificate, which can cause validation failures. You can upgrade to a newer version of Python to solve this. For more information, see https://urllib3.readthedocs.org/en/latest/security.html#snimissingwarning.
  SNIMissingWarning

```
Details for this warning can be found here: 
https://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advanced-usage.html#ssl-warnings

Easiest ways to supress this warning is to use python >= 2.7.9 (or disable those warnings from within python script itself).

This is actually not a bug, but a bit annoying. Moreover usage of SNI could be useful for some people anyway.
Everybody can easily change the Dockerfile himself, but maybe it's a good idea to increase python version in the provided Dockerfile directly.

Thank you!

issue_title: CALLLESS simuvex state option making up successors
issue_body:
The CALLLESS state option is set for the call_state at the first address of a function.  During execution I am looking at the path successors in order to determine if the path is in a loop or not (checking if it was already in the address trace).  Since the CALLLESS option overwrites all function calls, the path successors does not include the function addr.  However, this is an issue when it comes to a tail call at the end of a function.  Looking at the path successors it states that the next possible address (immediately following the function call) is a successor even though it isn't.  The CFG however does the analysis correctly and lists the function call as the only successor.

An example is for coreutils/true.c version 8.26 function: usage
In the CFG you can see in the bottom right corner the end of usage at address 0x4016fa.  There is no successor other than the function call (overwritten using CALLLESS during execution).  However, during analysis angr thinks that 0x401701 is a successor of 0x4016fa.

![cfg](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25495067/30704807-d5179296-9ec1-11e7-8029-36be03d21986.png)

issue_title: Branch on conditional move instructions
issue_body:
In the ARM64 binary that I'm analyzing, the conditional move instructions were used. For example:
```
47788          SUB             X10, X10, X23
4778C          CSEL            X12, X8, X10, NE
47790          BR              X12
```
It seems that angr does not know to branch into two after 0x47790. Therefore I wrote a hook as below:
```python
class HookCSEL(angr.SimProcedure):
    NO_RET = True
    def run(self):
        state = self.state
        copy = state.copy()
        addr1 = state.solver.eval(state.regs.x8)
        addr2 = state.solver.eval(state.regs.x10)
        print repr(state) + '\t' + hex(addr1) + '\t' + hex(addr2)
        state.regs.x12 = addr1
        copy.regs.x12 = addr2
        self.successors.add_successor(state, 0x47790, state.se.true, 'Ijk_Boring')
        self.successors.add_successor(copy, 0x47790, state.se.false, 'Ijk_Boring')
```
, in hope that it can help angr to branch out after 0x4778C in the control flow graph generated by CFGAccurate. However that did not work. Could someone please shed a light on this?

issue_title: search: citesummary quirks
issue_body:
Originally on 2010-12-03

There are 3 quirks/improvements to be done with citesummary format:

1) The links on the numbers in the citesummary tables now function correctly if the users uses advanced search, as per #340.  However, the links are generated by taking the users search and appending one or two terms in invenio syntax  (i.e. user searches 

```
Author:brooks 
```
and citesummary generates  
```
author:brooks and Collection:citeable and cited:50->100
```

This is fine.   But Invnenio syntax mixed with SPIRES syntax can be odd, and many (most?) people will be doing:

```
find a brooks, t
```
so the resulting links are
```
find a brooks, t and collection:citeable and cited:50->100
```

I have not seen any evidence of this failing, but mixing the syntaxes can lead to odd results, especially with authors.   This should be confirmed to work correctly and/or fixed to ensure it works.


2) We should add a link to "remove Review of Particle Properties from analysis" (we might check if one is in the results first)  We usually do this by ```
-title:rpp
```
since anything with title:rpp is assumed to be an RPP and thus off the carts as far as citation analysis goes.   People like to be able to remove these easily and redo the analysis.   We might check the result for possible RPPs and then display "This set includes at least one Review of Particle Properties, which is usually very highly cited, click here to remove from analysis"


3) When one follows the links off of the numbers, the results are sorted in "times cited order"   I think this is incorrect, and confusing to the user - particularly if one then wants to do another search using the regular search box.    It is too hard to see that the search order has been changed, and the new search is in a confusing order.    It is arguable whether the search from the citesummary link should be in times-cited order.   However, the fact that the users then needs to change the rank order (which is hard to find...) back to "or rank by" (which is not intuitive...) to get the standard search behavior is a bad problem, especially for the following use case:

Search a set of papers (say by a particular person)
Look at the top to see what they've done recently
Select citesummary to see their overall picture
Look at the few highly cited ones to see what they've done that was big (here sort barely matters)

Now, you have a good picture of the person, so you move to the next (reviewer/candidate/author/friend/whatever):

Search the set of papers
Look at the top to see what they have done recently....

oops, the top is now the highly cited papers by this person, how did that happen...

So I suggest that the links off the numbers should report in default sort order - by the principle of least surprise.  possibly one could solve this by  other means as well.     



see also #326 #134 for other changes to this popular format.

issue_title: Can no longer customize diffs
issue_body:
Prior to 64db01cf85f85f002a0ae80a5af4f6e2e6cc0abe it was possible to customize the way that the diff was presented for a `Resource` by overriding `get_diff()`. That commit replaced `get_diff()` with a hard-coded `Diff()` class.

One particular problem we have with `Diff()` is that because it forces an export representation of all the fields it can cause Django to issue additional queries to create the diff when the importing foreign keys, and that ruins the import performance.

We'd like to be able to disable the diff in those situations.

I propose that we replace the hard-coded `Diff` with a `get_diff_class` which defaults to returning `Diff`. Then we can customize as required.

I will make a PR.

issue_title: Meta exclude field not working
issue_body:
So i've tried to export my table without the ID column to make a test: if postgresql do not auto-increment only when i try to import a file with ID. The problem is the .CSV is not accepted to be import because it's missing the ID column. This occurs over the admin page and over the django's shell.

Over the admin page i got this for each row
```python
Line number: 1 - 'id'
Traceback (most recent call last):
File "/Users/vyscond/Tartarus/infosae-rest/.venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/import_export/resources.py", line 323, in import_data
instance, new = self.get_or_init_instance(instance_loader, row)
File "/Users/vyscond/Tartarus/infosae-rest/.venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/import_export/resources.py", line 154, in get_or_init_instance
instance = self.get_instance(instance_loader, row)
KeyError: 'id'
```

Here is my admin Object's
```python
class PersonResource(resources.ModelResource):
    class Meta:
        model = Person
        exclude = ('id',)
        fields = (
            'name' ,
            'address' ,
            'phone' ,
            )

class PersonAdmin(ImportExportActionModelAdmin):
    resource_class = PersonResource
    ordering = ('phone',)
```

So why the hell the we have the options to exclude if the 
```python
class ModelInstanceLoader(BaseInstanceLoader):
    """
    Instance loader for Django model.

    Lookup for model instance by ``import_id_fields``.
    """

    def get_queryset(self):
        return self.resource._meta.model.objects.all()

    def get_instance(self, row):
        try:
            params = {}
            for key in self.resource.get_import_id_fields():
                field = self.resource.fields[key]
                params[field.attribute] = field.clean(row)
            return self.get_queryset().get(**params)
        except self.resource._meta.model.DoesNotExist:
            return None
```

always try to read the ID? Where is this ID come from if it's possible to exclude it over the exporting process? This, of course, is related to the mysterious about what Django and Postgres do when we pass a not 0 value on an auto-increment field type like the default ID. For SQLite this is not a trouble, it's just accept the value and move on incrementing, but this is not the database we use in production, right?

1 - Whats the matter with the function of import having routines based on ID column (which is possible to be excluded)?
2 - How this library/django behaves about set a non-zero value for an auto-increment column/field on real databases?

issue_title: Hook to run pip-review on requirements.txt

issue_title: `Predictor.from_path` should take a `cuda_device` parameter
issue_body: so that you can run a predictor on a GPU without having to manually call `load_archive`

issue_title: Test failures on NFS drive
issue_body:
Our tests create and delete directories very rapidly, and this doesn't play well with high-latency NFS connections.  @roys174 ran into test failures when running this from his UW machine that has an NFS drive, where the failure was obviously due to NFS (couldn't delete the NFS metadata file in the test directory because the resource was busy).  Removing the delete inside of `tearDown` doesn't work, because some tests rely on having an empty `TMP_TEST/` directory when they start (e.g., tests that train something, because of our restart behavior).

Moving the test directory to `/tmp/allennlp_tests/` instead of `TMP_TEST/` _should_ fix the issue, because that will always use the local disk instead of NFS.  @joelgrus suggested `tempfile.mkdtemp()`, which should also work, particularly if you say the prefix is `/tmp`.

issue_title: Create a Dockerfile that includes a basic pip installation of AllenNLP

issue_title: Add something like npm watch in pipenv.
issue_body:
It is just my suggestion to add watch feature in pipenv that resembles npm watch, npm watch makes you able to run some scripts whenever you save for files after a change.

One of the use cases of this feature might be to run all your unit tests whenever you make changes to your files, thus giving you instant feedback about your changes.

And another use case could be to run static code analysis on your codebase whenever you make changes to your files.

I have been using pipenv for a while and I think this is a good feature for pipenv.

issue_title: Environment activation on the shebang line?
issue_body:
I was inspired to ask about specifying the project dir as a command line arg (https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/issues/2237) while thinking about invoking tools in their own venvs. At some point I realized that I could use a venv's `python` to invoke a tool and it would just work without having to activate anything my shell. I assume this is entirely by design.

With pipenv managing venv paths, it might be compelling to be able to invoke a tool via pipenv, where pipenv would determine the venv from the tool's path and not the current working directory. Perhaps pipenv could be the tool runner on the shebang line, something like this:

```
#!/usr/bin/env pipenv run

import mystuff
import sys

def main(args):
  # And so forth.
  return 0

if __name__ == '__main__':
  sys.exit(main(sys.argv[1:]))
```

Is this compelling? Or am I missing a different best practice for organizing tools with their own venvs? Or do people just prefer to not do this and use global site-packages for everything they intend to run on their own machines? (And if I'm dev'ing my own tools, I would do actual releases into my everyday-carry environment, and use venvs just for development?)

issue_title: Pipenv should support platform markers in pipenv install and pipenv install -r
issue_body:
`pipenv install "nsenter ; platform_system=='Linux'"`
should do the same as `pip install "..."`, and it should put this in your Pipfile:
`nsenter = { version = "*", markers = "platform_system=='Linux'" }`
The same should be supported when importing a requirements.txt using `pipenv install -r`.

```

Pipfile.lock not found, creating…

```

I did what it suggested: `pipenv install --skip-lock` and then `pipenv graph`

The graph shows that the only packages which ask for `html5lib` are dependencies of `tensorflow`, and they don't seem to conflict with each other or the installed version:

```
tensorflow==1.3.0
  - numpy [required: >=1.11.0, installed: 1.14.1]
  - protobuf [required: >=3.3.0, installed: 3.5.2]
    - setuptools [required: Any, installed: 38.5.1]

  - six [required: >=1.10.0, installed: 1.11.0]
  - tensorflow-tensorboard [required: >=0.1.0,<0.2.0, installed: 0.1.8]
    - bleach [required: ==1.5.0, installed: 1.5.0]
      - html5lib [required: >=0.999,<0.99999999,!=0.9999,!=0.99999, installed: 0.9999999]
        - six [required: Any, installed: 1.11.0]
      - six [required: Any, installed: 1.11.0]
    - html5lib [required: ==0.9999999, installed: 0.9999999]
      - six [required: Any, installed: 1.11.0]
    - markdown [required: >=2.6.8, installed: 2.6.11]
    - numpy [required: >=1.11.0, installed: 1.14.1]
    - protobuf [required: >=3.2.0, installed: 3.5.2]
      - setuptools [required: Any, installed: 38.5.1]
      - six [required: >=1.9, installed: 1.11.0]
    - six [required: >=1.10.0, installed: 1.11.0]
    - werkzeug [required: >=0.11.10, installed: 0.14.1]
    - wheel [required: >=0.26, installed: 0.30.0]
  - wheel [required: >=0.26, installed: 0.30.0]
```

So we have:
```
html5lib [required: >=0.999,<0.99999999,!=0.9999,!=0.99999, installed: 0.9999999]
```
and
```
html5lib [required: ==0.9999999, installed: 0.9999999]
```

That package uses a pretty horrible versioning scheme, but the required versions don't seem to conflict.

One requires `0.9999999` (seven zeros, what we have installed) and the other requires `<0.99999999` (eight zeros). Should be ok?

issue_title: Invalid environment marker: python_version
issue_body:
Attempting to install Nikola, I'm getting an error from `setuptools` that appeared to be coming from `doit`. While I'm not that familiar with the inner workings of pkg_resources, I took a look at the code referenced at the end of the stack trace, and my theory is that since the tests run in parallel, multiple threads may end up calling [_bypass_ensure_directory](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/pkg_resources/__init__.py#L3075-L3082) at the same time, and that the error stems from a file system race condition between `isdir` and `mkdir` when creating the directory, similar to in https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/pull/1083. Perhaps using the same solution could work here?

issue_body:
I have: virtualenv installed from pip(-2.7), python2.6 and python2.7 are also installed.

When I try to run
```
virtualenv --never-download --python=python2.6 env
```
it fails with this message:

```
Can't find any local distributions of setuptools to install and --never-download is set.  Either re-run virtualenv without the --never-download option, or place a setuptools distribution (setuptools-0.6c11-py2.6.egg) in one of these locations: ['.', '/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages', '/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/virtualenv_support', '/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/virtualenv_support']

I think the find_file_dirs method of virtualenv doesn't consider that virtualenv might be a python2.7 package, and therefore returns folders prefixed with ...python2.7
```

See also: Pull request in #203 for a possible fix

issue_title: Problem: Docs about getting Root URL of local Docker-based node are out-of-date
issue_body:
In the Python Driver docs page "[Determine the BigchainDB Root URL](https://docs.bigchaindb.com/projects/py-driver/en/latest/connect.html)", the last section is about how to determine the Root URL when running a local Docker-based node.

That section is out-of-date, referring to some old things, including another section in the docs:

https://docs.bigchaindb.com/projects/py-driver/en/latest/contributing.html#devenv-docker

The modern way to develop using Docker is to use the `make` commands in the BigchainDB Makefile, so the above-mentioned two sections in the docs should be updated to explain how to use that instead.

issue_title: Implement GET /transaction/<id>
Problem with while loop in Basic Usage Examples when done by script
issue_body:
This problem was reported by Thomas from Microsoft. I reproduced it on my machine.

* bigchaindb version: 0.8.0
* bigchaindb-driver version: 0.1.3
* Python version: 3.5.2
* Operating System: Ubuntu 14.04

### Setup

1. Install the versions as above.
2. Run RethinkDB and BigchainDB locally.
3. Run the following Python script (based on the Python Driver Basic Usage Examples):
```python
from bigchaindb_driver import BigchainDB
from bigchaindb_driver.crypto import generate_keypair

bdb = BigchainDB('http://localhost:9984/api/v1')

alice, bob = generate_keypair(), generate_keypair()
bicycle = {'data': {'bicycle': {'serial_number': 'abcd1234','manufacturer': 'bkfab'}}}
metadata = {'planet': 'earth'}

prepared_creation_tx = bdb.transactions.prepare(operation='CREATE', owners_before=alice.verifying_key, asset=bicycle, metadata=metadata)

fulfilled_creation_tx = bdb.transactions.fulfill(prepared_creation_tx, private_keys=alice.signing_key)

print(fulfilled_creation_tx)

sent_creation_tx = bdb.transactions.send(fulfilled_creation_tx)

txid = fulfilled_creation_tx['id']

print(txid)

print(sent_creation_tx == fulfilled_creation_tx)

trials = 0
while bdb.transactions.status(txid).get('status') != 'valid' and trials < 100:
    trials += 1

print(trials)
print(bdb.transactions.status(txid))
```

### What Happened

Here's my terminal output:
```bash
(p35bd) troy@ascribe3-XPS-8700:~/repos/bigchaindb-driver$ python ~/Desktop/test2.py 
{'transaction': {'operation': 'CREATE', 'metadata': {'data': {'planet': 'earth'}, 'id': '697fea59-dfad-484e-9e59-4e4b43e9d4b7'}, 'asset': {'updatable': False, 'refillable': False, 'divisible': False, 'data': {'bicycle': {'serial_number': 'abcd1234', 'manufacturer': 'bkfab'}}, 'id': '28d76212-02e2-423d-af2d-0c5a6e3065ad'}, 'fulfillments': [{'input': None, 'fulfillment': 'cf:4:xoZK0HdlKwORY2snn74er3HUZKVNSTJyXMDoQRTGQhqgZbpS9wn8d2wuYom1gEhDfY88d9dUNkx8iO8ZSAXQ1el0WeMuLFqu3EO9Yt3-p2NXqAl6T88dPYRQOrMP1rgN', 'owners_before': ['EMxVb7Eovrn6RZMRSZuwBzw8YaXJ4WVeRVqgvUQuNbhT'], 'fid': 0}], 'conditions': [{'amount': 1, 'cid': 0, 'condition': {'details': {'type': 'fulfillment', 'bitmask': 32, 'signature': None, 'public_key': 'EMxVb7Eovrn6RZMRSZuwBzw8YaXJ4WVeRVqgvUQuNbhT', 'type_id': 4}, 'uri': 'cc:4:20:xoZK0HdlKwORY2snn74er3HUZKVNSTJyXMDoQRTGQho:96'}, 'owners_after': ['EMxVb7Eovrn6RZMRSZuwBzw8YaXJ4WVeRVqgvUQuNbhT']}]}, 'version': 1, 'id': '137f0f88307517bbd72a94b84ea7aaba0267970efc572346d6cd42f2bae0cc42'}
137f0f88307517bbd72a94b84ea7aaba0267970efc572346d6cd42f2bae0cc42
True
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/troy/Desktop/test2.py", line 25, in <module>
    while bdb.transactions.status(txid).get('status') != 'valid' and trials < 100:
  File "/home/troy/repos/bigchaindb-driver/bigchaindb_driver/driver.py", line 213, in status
    return self.transport.forward_request(method='GET', path=path)
  File "/home/troy/repos/bigchaindb-driver/bigchaindb_driver/transport.py", line 46, in forward_request
    response = connection.request(method=method, path=path, json=json)
  File "/home/troy/repos/bigchaindb-driver/bigchaindb_driver/connection.py", line 45, in request
    raise exc_cls(response.status_code, text, json)
bigchaindb_driver.exceptions.NotFoundError: (404, '{\n  "message": "Not found", \n  "status": 404\n}\n', {'message': 'Not found', 'status': 404})
```

I'm not exactly sure what's going on. Obviously a transaction with that `txid` wasn't found, and a `bigchaindb_driver.exceptions.NotFoundError` was raised, but why? What happened to the transaction?

If you wait long enough, an exception is _not_ raised. Instead, one gets back `'valid``.

There are two issues here:

1. What happened to the transaction that cause it to be momentarily "not found"?
2. The while loop should keep going even if the transaction is not found, i.e. the exception shouldn't cause the while loop to terminate.

Note: This issue may be related to #174 but it's not the same.

issue_title: Error in `python': munmap_chunk(): invalid pointer
issue_body:

"Container header CRC32 failure"

I fired up a python command line (2.7.11) GCC 4.4.7 201220313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-1)

issue_title: pysam.FastaFile.fetch() does not consist of "0-based coordinates" for the end position
issue_body:
According to the manual, pysam.FastaFile.fetch() "*...consists of reference name (‘chr1’), start (100000), and end (200000). 0-based coordinates*". After an implementation based on this scentence I realized that sequences were too short.

Then I tested the following:

\>>> p = pysam.FastaFile("<somefastafile>.fa")
\>>> p.fetch("<some chromosome>",0,0)
''
\>>> p.fetch("<some chromosome>",0,1)
'G'

This means that only the start is 0-based and the end-position is 1-based (0-based **half open**?). Would it be possible to change the documentation and provide an example to illustrate this?

issue_title: Pileup region calculating 100 flanking bases by default
issue_body:
Following the documentation example I want to retrieve pileup from 197396820 to 197396822 bases of chr1. 
```python
    samfile = pysam.AlignmentFile('myfile.bam', 'rb')
    for pileupcolumn in samfile.pileup("chr1", 197396820, 197396822):
        print ("\ncoverage at base %s = %s" % (pileupcolumn.pos, pileupcolumn.n))
```

However output I'm getting is pileup from position 197396720 to 197396921 (100 flanking bases above and below specified region).

Here's start, interest region (middle) and end of the output I'm getting:
```
coverage at base 197396720 = 1
coverage at base 197396721 = 5
coverage at base 197396722 = 13
...
coverage at base 197396820 = 429
coverage at base 197396821 = 433
...
coverage at base 197396918 = 20
coverage at base 197396919 = 15
coverage at base 197396920 = 9
coverage at base 197396921 = 4
```

I would like to know why I'm getting pileups not between start and end coordinates for the specified chromosome.

Inspecting pysam pileup code I guess I need to pass kwarg `truncate`, to achieve my goal, however I can't make it work. I've been trying this:
```python
for item in samfile.pileup(reference="chr1", start=197396820, end=197396822, **{"truncate": True}):
   print item
  File "calignmentfile.pyx", line 3421, in pysam.calignmentfile.PileupColumn.__str__ (pysam/calignmentfile.c:36165)
AttributeError: 'pysam.calignmentfile.PileupColumn' object has no attribute 'nsegmentes'
```

issue_title: output read coverage for all bases, even uncovered ones
issue_body:
Hi, 

I would like to create a numpy array with read coverage information for each bases of a whole chromosome, even uncovered region (depth = 0), to be able to retrieve a base coverage with the array indices.

I tried pysam's pileup function, but it seems to only output information for covered regions:

```
bamfile = pysam.AlignmentFile(bamFile, "rb" )
itr = samfile.pileup("chr22")
cov = np.fromiter([c.n for c in itr], int)
```

With samtools I can retrieve all bases coverage with: 
`samtools depth -aa *.bam`
or
` pysam.depth("-aa", "*.bam")` 

The pysam depth is clearly inefficient as I have to parse the output to get the coverage...

Is there a way to do it with pysam in an efficient way like with pileup function?

Thanks in advance for your help,
Maxime

issue_title: provide subscription topic in event details for non-pattern-based subscriptions
issue_body:
Currently the subscription handler receives the event details as they come over the wire. For non-pattern-based subscriptions, the subscription topic is not sent over the wire since the client has the knowledge. Consequently the subscription details do not provide the topic.

This is unexpected behavior, since it exposes a wire-level optimization to the user.

In Autobahn|JS, the subscription topic is provided for all events. 

Change Autobahn|Python to have the same behavior.

issue_title: Is 'stop_gradient' supported?
issue_body:
Can we mark a variable as a constant value, i.e do not propagate derivative calculation further?

For example, we have:
```
y = g(x) = 2 * x
z = f(x, y) = x * y
```
and we want to evaluate `\frac{\partial z}{\partial x}` which is supposed to be `y` (and its value is 2*x)  rather than 4*x (which is `\frac{\partial x * 2 * x}{\partial x}`).

So how to mark `y` or `g(x)` as 'gradient back propagation is stopped here'?

Thanks,
Gang

issue_title: Looking for best practice tips
issue_body:
Hi, 

We're using pykafka in production for our analytics systems, and have found it good. We were using kafka-python earlier, found issues with MultiKafkaConsumer and also data loss issues. 

I wanted to get some advice from the community. The use case is something like this: A single input topic and multiple output topics. The consumer will consume from the input topic, do a bunch of stuff and delegate it to a producer which puts them into corresponding output topic. 

We want to have an array of Kafka producers, which will pick an event E, classify it into E1, E2 etc., do a bunch of transformations and put them into individual event topics. Eg. The input topic is a clickstream topic where all events come in, and from there all login events go to a login topic, all signup events go to a signup topic, etc. How to do this efficiently?

Also is it a good practice to do `with topic.get_producer() as producer` each time we want to produce an event from a running stream of events? 

Right now, we're doing something like this:

```
producer = topic.get_producer
def send(event):
      producer.produce(event)
```

, where send() is called by a consumer constantly consuming events. Here the single producer object is used for sending all the events. 

Hoping for some help here, 
Thanks!

issue_title: Replace all usage of sphinx :param: lists
issue_body:
In our docstrings, a number of things are still documented using the Sphinx parameter lists, as in
```python
"""
:param name: Foo a bar with ``name``!
:param othervar: Baz a biz with ``othervar``?
"""
```

These were fine as long as we kept our descriptions extremely succinct, but more and more information has been creeping into these lists. Eventually, the rendering breaks down and we get weird strings like

> Para-
> meters: ...

To resolve, just switch to the newer style docstrings I've started using:

```
"""
**Parameters**

    ``param_name``
      Description text
"""
```

issue_title: The computation of statistics runs out of memory for large classical calculations
issue_body:
This is the case of the SHARE model. 128 GB are not enough to keep in memory the curves for all realizations. There are 3200 of them, with 126,044 points and 312 levels, which means that at least 937 GB are required at 8 bytes per float.
The solution is to perform the computation of the statistics in tiles. Also, since the computation is heavy it is better to parallelize it. Notice that the complication is due solely to the quantiles, for the mean we could easily use a memory-saving algorithm.

    with open(model_dump_path+"classes_and_length", "w") as data_details_file:
        data_details_file_payload = json.dumps({"number_of_classes":number_of_classes, "max_input_length":max_input_length, "input_dimention":input_dimention})
        data_details_file.write(data_details_file_payload)

    # print(trainX)
    # print(trainY)

    testX, testY = trainX, trainY

    # Sequence padding
    trainX = pad_sequences(trainX, maxlen=max_input_length, value=0.)
    testX = pad_sequences(testX, maxlen=max_input_length, value=0.)
    # Converting labels to binary vectors
    trainY = to_categorical(trainY, nb_classes=number_of_classes)
    testY = to_categorical(testY, nb_classes=number_of_classes)

    tf.reset_default_graph()

    # Network building
    net = tflearn.input_data([None, max_input_length])
    net = tflearn.embedding(net, input_dim=input_dimention, output_dim=128)
    net = tflearn.lstm(net, 128, dropout=0.2, return_seq=True, restore=True)
    net = tflearn.lstm(net, 128, dropout=0.3, return_seq=True, restore=True)
    net = tflearn.lstm(net, 128, dropout=0.4, return_seq=False, restore=True)
    net = tflearn.fully_connected(net, number_of_classes, activation='softmax', restore=True)
    net = tflearn.regression(net, optimizer='adam', learning_rate=0.001,
                             loss='categorical_crossentropy', restore=True)

    # Training
    model = tflearn.DNN(net, tensorboard_verbose=0)

    model_file_path = model_dump_path+'model.tflearn'

    if os.path.exists(model_dump_path+'checkpoint'):
        print("Loading existig model for posttraning")
        model.load(model_file_path)
        # >> model.load("model.tfl.ckpt-500")
        print("Loaded existing model")

    model.fit(trainX, trainY, n_epoch=epochs, snapshot_step=1000, shuffle=True,
              validation_set=0.3, show_metric=True, batch_size=64)

    print("Finished training")

    model.save(model_file_path)

    print("Saved model")
```

issue_title: Support for Overpass API /api/status
issue_body:
<!--- Verify first that your issue/request has not already been reported -->

##### Issue type
<!--- Pick one below and delete the rest: -->
 - Feature Idea

##### OverPy version
<!--- Paste verbatim output from “python -c 'import overpy; print(overpy.__version__)'” or the hash of the git commit between quotes below. -->
```
0.4
```

##### OS
<!---
Add information about the os you are using OverPy on.
Pick one below and delete the rest:
-->
 - Windows 7

#### Python version
<!-- Pick at least one below and delete the rest -->
 - Python 3.6

##### Summary
<!--- Explain the problem briefly. -->
It is possible to encounter several HTTP errors when using the Overpass API, relating to the status of a user's queries.  For experienced users, helpful information is available regarding these errors from [/api/status](http://overpass-api.de/api/status).  The ability to catch and access the information from `/api/status` would be beneficial to users trying to make a significant number of queries while respecting the rules of the Overpass servers.

<!--- You can also add links to gist.github.com. -->

##### Expected results
<!--- What did you expect to happen? -->

Something like the following would be expected:
```python
query_string = ('way["highway"~"."](around:20,{},{},);out;').format(lat, lon)
    while True:
        try:
            result = api.query(query_string)
            break
        except (overpy.exception.AnyOverpassError):
            print(result.api_status)
```
Which gives some output:
```python
'Connected as: 2557965506
Current time: 2017-05-01T16:17:38Z
Rate limit: 2
2 slots available now.
Currently running queries (pid, space limit, time limit, start time):'
```
As found on the `/api/status` site.

This would allow users to see information like rate limit and available slots, and adjust the code to respect the status of the server.

issue_title: Scalar graphics: x axis doesn't represent `global_step` values
issue_body:
Hi! 
I've got strange behavior of scalar graphics. 
The X axis doesn't represent `global_step` values, which are set in `FileWriter.add_summary()` method.

**Here's a small example (from Jupyter Notebook):**

```
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np

!rm ./test/*
tf.reset_default_graph()

pl = tf.placeholder(tf.int64, (), 'pl')
res = tf.add(pl, 1)
tf.summary.scalar('res', res)

summary = tf.summary.merge_all()

writer = tf.summary.FileWriter('./test', graph=tf.get_default_graph())        
with tf.Session() as sess:
    sess.run(tf.global_variables_initializer())

    for i in range(100):
        _res, _summary = sess.run([res, summary],
                                   feed_dict={pl:i})
        writer.add_summary(_summary, global_step=i)

!tensorboard --logdir ./test/ 
```

**And I see this in TensorBoard:**

![scr-0](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/6130692/43677424-77f23a6a-980a-11e8-81fa-77a43bfa27e3.png)

As you can see, x axis takes values from `[0, 78]` instead of `[0, 100]`. And  if you add more operations to the graph, x axis range will change.
Also, if you set `range(50)` (for example) for `i`, graphic will not be displayed at all

**Additional info:**

- TensorFlow, TensorBoard version: 1.9.0
- OS: Ubuntu Linux 16.04
- Python: 3.6.2

- `fa-clock` for Datetime/timestamp

- `fa-check` for boolean
- `fa-quote-left` for string

- fa-string: alias for `fa-quote-left` or `fa-quote-right`
- fa-number: alias for `fa-hashtag`
- fa-boolean: `Y/N` or just alias for `fa-check`
- fa-int: `123`
- fa-float: `1.2`
- fa-array: `[ ]` or `[..]`
- fa-object: `{ }` or `{..}`
- fa-binary:  ![binary](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/181567/16350416/6cc3474e-3a13-11e6-9a1e-25a8eee34fb6.png) (nevermind my horrible design skills)

issue_title: List parameters and their annotations in `global` section with `-h` option
issue_body:
So suppose I have this workflow:

```
$ sos run workflows/fastqtl_to_mash.ipynb -h -v3


Available workflows
  export
  default
  convert

Sections
  convert_0:           Generate utility functions
  convert_1:           Convert summary stats gzip format to HDF5
  convert_2:           Merge single study data to multivariate data
  default_3:           Extract data to fit MASH model
  default_4:           Compute for MASH EZ model and save to RDS
  export:              Export notebook to HTML file
```

it neatly lists all steps in the workflow. But under `[global]` I have something like this:

```
parameter: cwd = path('./gtex6_workflow_output')
parameter: data_list = path("data/eQTL_summary_files.txt")
parameter: gene_list = path()
parameter: msg = "GTEx eQTL summary statistics"
# maximum number of groups per HDF5 file
parameter: maxsize = 1000 
parameter: cols = [8, 9, 7]
parameter: keep_ensg_version = 0
# number of null samples per gene to train MASH mixture
parameter: null_per_gene = 9 
# a list of effect names (SNP names) to exclude from mash analysis
parameter: exclude_mash_effects_list = 'NULL' 
# a list of condition names (tissue names) to include in mash output. 
# Default includes all conditions.
parameter: include_mash_condition_list = 'NULL' 
# size of MASH train set
parameter: mash_train_size = 20000 
```

Correct me if I am wrong, but I thought we can list parameters with `-v`? I do not think it works, though. It would be great if we can list parameters along with what they are, eg:

```
--exclude_mash_effects_list: a list of effect names (SNP names) to exclude from mash analysis
```

Not sure if `#` is the best identifier for parameter comments to show, but we can also do `##` or `#'` (`roxygen` convention). I'm just saying that showing parameter options could be useful when I make quick software-type of workflows for others to run without looking into the details.

issue_title: NumPy error when no descriptors are found in ORB
issue_body:
## Description

When you pass a blurry image to an ORB object's `detect_and_extract` method, and it finds no keypoints, it crashes with an error inside of `np.hstack` because `scales_list` is empty:

```python
extractor_em.detect_and_extract(simlight)
```

```pytb
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
IndexError                                Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-37-caefda1c8359> in <module>()
----> 1 extractor_em.detect_and_extract(simlight8)

/Users/jni/conda/envs/ana3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/skimage/feature/orb.py in detect_and_extract(self, image)
    321         keypoints = np.vstack(keypoints_list)
    322         responses = np.hstack(responses_list)
--> 323         scales = np.hstack(scales_list)
    324         orientations = np.hstack(orientations_list)
    325         descriptors = np.vstack(descriptors_list).view(np.bool)

/Users/jni/conda/envs/ana3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/numpy/core/shape_base.py in hstack(tup)
    275     arrs = [atleast_1d(_m) for _m in tup]
    276     # As a special case, dimension 0 of 1-dimensional arrays is "horizontal"
--> 277     if arrs[0].ndim == 1:
    278         return _nx.concatenate(arrs, 0)
    279     else:

IndexError: list index out of range
```

## Way to reproduce

Using the image attached below, run:

```python
from skimage import io, feature
im = io.imread('simlight.png')
extractor_im = feature.ORB(n_keypoints=50)
extractor_em.detect_and_extract(simlight8)
```

Affects skimage master. The problem is in [this `if` clause](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/58e613e49677cf5a25d23abd6c13cf5ec0a880e0/skimage/feature/orb.py#L305), which updates all the necessary lists with empty arrays, *except* `scales_list`.

![simlight](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/492549/17913343/af8b22b0-69dc-11e6-90de-269bbbe01178.png)

issue_title: Address Healthcheck Synchronization
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

The health check task and the greenlet responsible for a node message queue are not synchronized.

## Solution

Instead of using a Health Check task for all nodes, we could add the ping logic in the `_send_queued_message`.

Example pseudo-code:

```
Send ping to check if partner is online:
    - if ping unconfirmed
        - node is unavailable
        - wait for partner to come online (if any message is received from partner)
loop:
    - message := queue.peek with time-out after send-ping-time
    - if message is None
        - message := Ping
        - wait for confirmation or max_unresponsive_time
        - if message unconfirmed
            - node is unavailable
            - wait for partner to come back online
    - while queue not empty
        - message := queue.pop, retries_left := max_retries
        - while message unconfirmed
            - send message
            - retries_left := retries_left -1
            - wait for confirmation or timeout
            - if retries_left == 0 node is unavailable # the message worked as the Ping
                - wait for partner to come back online
                - retries_left := max_retries
```

This would make one task per channel, eliminate synchronizations issues for messages that have max_retries, and we can start these queues in a randomization loop as suggested by @konradkonrad

issue_title: Designing OAuth2 client support
issue_body:
Thought I'd get some discussion going on how to best approach the versatility of OAuth2 client profiles and token types. While OAuth 2 is a fair bit simpler than OAuth1 I still think having several Client classes would be advantageous. Mainly because each client comes with its own set of "quirks" in the request/response flow.  Also, unlike OAuth1 extensibility plays a major role in OAuth2 and I think it would be easy to add extension client classes this way. For example the [Google Service Accounts grant model](https://developers.google.com/accounts/docs/OAuth2ServiceAccount). Furthermore, having several clients make it more explicit that there are differences between them, this is very important from a security point of view because although they may look similar they are built on vastly different security premises (im looking at you auth code and implicit grant). 

My idea for how these clients might look is based on thinly wrapping #18. The clients share enough functionality to work well of a base class with 3 polymorhpic methods. 

```
Client(object):
    # the base client, has the ability to add "bearer" and "mac" tokens

    def __init__(self, ....):
        # sets up token type and related values

    def add_token(self, uri, http_method=u'GET', body=None, headers=None):
        # attaches tokens either to uri, auth header or body in the case of
        # bearer tokens, and to auth header if mac token
        # this is a very basic operation for both token types

    def validate_token_params(self, ...):
        # param validation is the same for all client profiles but highly
        # recommended by the spec for security reasons

    def prepare_token_uri(self, ...):
        raise NotImplementedError(..)

    def parse_token_uri_response(self, response):
        # extracts and validates params from response
        # params are added to self 
        # abstract because it differs in implicit/auth code
        raise NotImplementedError(..) 

    def prepare_token_body(self, ...):
        raise NotImplementedError(..)

    def parse_token_body_response(self, response):
        # extracts and validates params from response
        # params are added to self   
        # not abstract since its the same for all profiles

ConfidentialClient(Client):
    # follows the authorization code grant model
    # overrides all abstract methods:
    # prepare_token_uri, parse_token_uri_response, prepare_token_body

PublicClient(Client):
    # follows the implicit code grant model
    # overrides prepare_token_uri & parse_token_uri_response

CredentialsClient(Client):
    # follows the client credentials grant model
    # overrides prepare_token_body

PasswordClient(Client):
    # follows the resource owner password credentials grant
    # overrides prepare_token_body
```

Any input on how to properly name the parse-and-populate methods would be awesome. Oh, and remember that the responses for OAuth2 are clearly defined and thus easily parsable =)
